author: @cruzemissile5409
text: They still technically have an axle that is a bearing race with a large diameter but the promlem is the large diameter race means the balls inside have to spin faster around the outside of the larger inner and outer hub increasing friction and allowing slower maximum RPM without exploding or rapidly wearing out the bearng races from increased rpm and centrifugal forces. Larg wheels wiht smaller axle diameter are actually better as long as they are large enough to handle the over all load.

author: @TechTeacher222
text:
￼Seen this?

It’s a story we’ve heard so many times over the years: breathless reporting of a new scientific breakthrough that will deliver limitless power, energy storage, or whichever other of humanity’s problems needs solving today. Sadly, they so often fail to make the jump into our daily lives because the reporting glosses over some exotic material that costs a fortune or because there’s a huge issue elsewhere in their makeup. There’s a story from MIT that might just be the real thing, though, as a team from that university claim to have made a viable supercapacitor from materials as simple as cement, carbon black, and a salt solution.

Supercapacitors use plate materials with a huge surface area on which to store charge. Conventional supercapacitors often use an electrochemical construction, and activated charcoal is a frequent electrode material. The cement capacitor uses the property of cement curing, which creates a dense branching network of openings in the material as the water reacts with the cement. By introducing electrically conductive carbon black to the mix and using potassium chloride solution instead of water, they turn the huge surface area of the resulting structure into a conductive electrode suffused with charged ions. This can be used as a plate of a supercapacitor, separated from another similar one by a membrane.

The suggestion is that, in the future, the foundation of a house or other structure might be cast in this cement to provide in-situ energy storage for rooftop solar generation. There’s an environmental question over the carbon footprint of cement manufacturing. Still, since the materials and techniques appear neither exotic nor expensive, we hope this is the one energy storage miracle discovery that makes it.

author: @eXeYeZe-404
text:
Would raising your toy top generator 7
Unit up and dropping it along the shaft provide enough force to add gearing that could amplify the speed for increased rotation at the rim. Say having a 2 meter shaft and about a 20 kilo flywheel instead of 2 kilos. Then I believe you could generate as the weight moved down the shaft and as the cable used to lift it could also generated from the motor used to lift it I think that could get 2 generating points off of 1 gravity battery. I wish I could send you a drawing of what I seein my mind. Or even having 2 or more twisted guide rails and 1 lifting motor with each guide rail having an individual generator. I'm a homeless vet here in Illinois staying with friends kind enough to let me stay with them. I have limited funds being on disability and space to build anything. But do enjoy the ideas you  share immensely.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i am not sure you need to raise the whole generator mate - but i am making a model to demonstrate my Idea of how to turn this into a gravity generator

replies.author: @eXeYeZe-404
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering my thoughts is instead of just using a dead weight for the energy storage, a very torque dependent generator that was simple but heavy that requires the force from the heavy weight to drive some kind of gearing system that could produce incredibly hight speeds. In your videos you're always mentioning how to produce higher speeds thru gearing. I do wish I could send you a rough sketch of what I'm imagining. Basically it's a stack of planetary gears where the central axel is twisted like in your spinning top. So as the generator drops down the sheer mass could drive the low end of the gearing to produce very high speeds at the end of the gear stack. Or since raising the weight may be an issue, changing the twisted guide rail(s) some kind of toothed rail(s) that the motor\ generator could climb and then produce on the way down. It might not be the best way but I'm think about the small footprint of less than a square meter for as tall as possible. In urban environments space is an issue. I lived in Atlanta for over 20 years so a gravity battery attached to the side of any building could be be easily added to keep it from being an eye sore. Brick it up and looks like a chimney. Use the same siding or paint on the enclosure and it should blend in like urban camouflage. Thanks for writing back. I've been binge watching your videos at night for months. I'll go comment on the ones that really interest me. I hope the positive vibes you put out will be received well by all.

author: @JonLovestheLord
text: Hmmm, it's not exactly 'hubless' really, is it?  The axle is expanded out to the rim, but it still provides the support for the frame.  It is essentially a fixed axle expanded out radially into a very large tube; unless you are defining an axle as a free rotation pivot bar that is able to spin independent of the frame.  The same concept would work with a large tubular bushing as the freely moving 'axle' at the hub rim, like an inner bearing race.

replies.author: @danedmiston9673
replies.text: I always though of the hub as the part that supports the wheel/rotor and holds the bearings.  What is being described in the video is a rotating device with a large hollow shaft with very large bearing that still needs a hub.   When I think "hub-less" I think of tank tracks with several rollers supporting the "wheels".

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sure why not

author: @grantofat6438
text: If you have to support it at the rim, won't that result in more friction than using an axle? Usually the aim is to have as small a contact point as possible, like you demonstrated with ball point bearings. Now you are going in the exact opposite direction. It seems inefficient to me.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: depends on how you support it

replies.author: @grantofat6438
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Then show how you would support it at the rim so it runs as freely as with an axle.

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text:
The NIMBY effect - applies to lots of undesirable things. Wind turbines, prisons, sewage works, low-cost housing developments. People generally approve of building these things in the abstract, so long as they are being built somewhere else - but as soon as they learn the next one is being built close to their home, suddenly the objections start.

We had quite a campaign around my place for a new river crossing. We need one, everyone agrees on that - right now it's a very long journey upstream to reach the nearest bridge and come back again, and that crossing is often congested. We got it down to three possibilities - and each location had a campaign running arguing that surely theirs was the worst, and the other two were far better options. Because we all want to live near to the crossing, but no-one wants to live right next door to it to face extra traffic and the disruption associated with construction and road-widening.

replies.author: @davidprocter3578
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering yes never claimed to be anything other !!! Sorry did not explain myself very well. the concrete ring only there as a base and a shed, the roof of the shed is not so much a roof but a large diameter wind turbine, that just so happens to keep out the weather. it is the combining of a static coil and mag lev that gets complicated to explain but it is your thoughts on various blades that has distracted me. as I had set in my mind just how the wind turbine would look. 
Why shed , so as not to waste space in modest gardens, it would also provide space for batteries and  all the usual gubbins required these days. out side the bounds of regulation is certainly a major plus for those wanting to reduce energy bills. At the size I suggest even in low wind speed areas there should be enough wind power generated energy to offset lighting and refrigeration mobiles and lap tops

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: @vylbird8014A solar concentrator or wind turbine for decentralized electricity and heat generation is barely noticeable if you aren't looking for excuses to nitpick. Not that different from satellite dishes and TV antennas.

author: @richardmccann4815
text: We are far too slow at this sort of simple engineering, and far too fast at wasting time and money in the bottomless pits of nuclear power and fusion technologies!  Can't wait for the next video!

author: @industrialmonk
text: Hubless might be an interesting idea but the engineering complexity is extreme as you have seen with your 3d print tolerances & as for vehicles there's a problem it's unsprung Whight & a hubless wheel will always be heavier & this problem will the suspension more complex & the ride comfort reduced

replies.author: @CruorEtFerrum
replies.text: All very serious points, add in the torque flex mounting problem of a giant hollow axle and outer bearing race stress/flex, the weight savings starts evaporating fast. Toss in the costs of ridiculously expensive bearings for this "hubless" wonder...  Big globo corps love the no maintenance possible throw away model, be careful of what you wish for vs your needs.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's funny mate - just a few days ago I was looking at a production electric vehicles that had four hub motors

replies.author: @CruorEtFerrum
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering  Love the AWD aspect of 4 hub motors.  How about planetary drives? Pain to work on but cool to me and minimal power train. Waiting for a motor and magnetic transmission stuffed into a planetary hub for durability on heavier equipment. Gotta admit the hollow looking wheel does look cool though, even with its particular limits.

replies.author: @industrialmonk
replies.text: @CruorEtFerrum yes thay do look extremely cool you will remember the light cycle from Tron I would love one but as a hand's on engineer thay intrigue & scar the hell out of me at the same time. I love science & experimenting. keep up the good work
